channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: JWST maps the weather on a hot gas giant 700 light-years away - Ars Technica
item.description:
1. JWST maps the weather on a hot gas giant 700 light-years away  Ars Technica

  3. Astronomers de-fog exoplanet atmospheres with new cloud-detecting method  Johns Hopkins University
  4. This exoplanet's sky is full of puffy clouds made of vaporized rock—but only on one side  Scientific American
  5. Webb Telescope Detects Cloudy Mornings And Clear Nights On Alien World  Forbes

item.title: A joint European-Chinese satellite just went up on Vega-C, and the images it returns could change how scientists understand Earth’s magnetic shield - Space Daily

item.title: Discovery of new fossils in Northwest Canada changes view of early animal evolution - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Discovery of new fossils in Northwest Canada changes view of early animal evolution  Phys.org
  2. The origin of sex is a 567 million-year-old deep-sea creature  The Times
  3. These bizarre fossils represent some of the earliest moving, sexually reproducing life ever discovered  Scientific American
  4. Complex animals evolved up to 10 million years earlier than previously thought, fossil discovery shows  Live Science
  5. Complex animal life may be 10 million years older than scientists thought — and a new fossil site in northwestern Canada suggests it all started in the deep sea, not the shallow waters where most early fossils show up  Space Daily

item.title: Scientists just reversed about 80% of aging in elderly mice in a single month — and they did it by boosting one protein - Space Daily

item.title: A few rocks spotted near a pond in northeastern Thailand in 2016 have just been confirmed as a 27-tonne, 27-metre sauropod — the largest dinosaur ever found in Southeast Asia - Space Daily
item.description:
1. A few rocks spotted near a pond in northeastern Thailand in 2016 have just been confirmed as a 27-tonne, 27-metre sauropod — the largest dinosaur ever found in Southeast Asia  Space Daily

  3. Giant new dinosaur identified from fossils in Thailand  BBC
  4. A Man Spotted Strange-Looking Rocks Near a Pond in Thailand. They Turned Out to Be the Bones of a Massive New Dinosaur Species  Smithsonian Magazine
  5. The first sauropod dinosaur from the Lower Cretaceous Khok Kruat Formation of Thailand enriches the diversity of somphospondylan titanosauriforms in southeast Asia | Scientific Reports  Nature

  5. Scientists may have found a hidden shortcut to moon that could cut spacecraft fuel costs  The Indian Express

item.title: There is a single satellite launched by the US Navy in 1964 that is still in orbit, still transmitting, and still being used by amateur radio operators around the world — and nobody at the Navy has been in charge of it for decades - Space Daily
item.description: There is a single satellite launched by the US Navy in 1964 that is still in orbit, still transmitting, and still being used by amateur radio operators around the world — and nobody at the Navy has been in charge of it for decades  Space Daily

item.title: The most accurate atomic clocks in operation now lose less than one second every 30 billion years — and the reason this matters isn't precision for its own sake, it's that gravity itself slows time slightly, and these clocks are now sensitive enough to measure the - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The most accurate atomic clocks in operation now lose less than one second every 30 billion years — and the reason this matters isn't precision for its own sake, it's that gravity itself slows time slightly, and these clocks are now sensitive enough to measure the  Space Daily
  2. Schrödinger’s clock: Time could tick faster and slower at the same time  ScienceDaily
  3. "Different Flows of Time All Exist at the Same Moment": Scientists Claim Trapped-Ion Atomic Clocks Can Observe “Quantum Superposition of Time”  The Debrief
  4. New atomic clocks can be used to observe the 'quantum superposition of time'  Earth.com
  5. Using atomic clocks to reveal the quantum properties of time  EurekAlert!

item.title: Space debris is forcing satellites to dodge more often — costing us vital science. 'Things will get worse before they get better' - Space
item.description:

  2. Scientists warn of heightened risk from rockets and satellite tumbling down on Earth  WION
  3. Study finds 47% of tracked orbital objects are uncontrolled junk  NewsBytes
  4. Earth's Orbit is a Real Junkyard: Almost Every Second Object in Space Has Already Turned into Debris  Наша Ніва
  5. Almost half of what is orbiting Earth is space junk  geographical.co.uk

item.title: Scott Kelly spent a year in orbit while his identical twin brother stayed on Earth, and when he came home NASA discovered his gene expression had changed in ways that didn't fully reverse - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Scott Kelly spent a year in orbit while his identical twin brother stayed on Earth, and when he came home NASA discovered his gene expression had changed in ways that didn't fully reverse  Space Daily
  2. Special Relativity’s Twin Paradox Explained – And How It's Resolved  IFLScience
  3. The ISS travels at 17,500 miles per hour, which means astronauts inside it are aging measurably slower than people on the ground, and by the time Scott Kelly returned from his year in orbit he was 5 milliseconds younger than his identical twin brother Mark.  Space Daily
  4. The International Space Station is travelling at 17,500 mph as you read this, and the astronauts inside it are aging measurably slower than you are  Space Daily

item.title: The Parker Solar Probe is still flying through the Sun's corona at 430,000 miles per hour, fast enough to cross the continental United States in about 20 seconds, because a 4.5-inch carbon-foam shield keeps the spacecraft in the shade - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The Parker Solar Probe is still flying through the Sun's corona at 430,000 miles per hour, fast enough to cross the continental United States in about 20 seconds, because a 4.5-inch carbon-foam shield keeps the spacecraft in the shade  Space Daily
  2. The Parker Solar Probe is now moving at 430,000 miles per hour, fast enough to cross the continental United States in 20 seconds, and it survives skimming the Sun's corona because a four-and-a-half-inch slab of carbon foam stays at room temperature on the  Space Daily
  3. NASA sent a spacecraft toward the sun - then the impossible happened  MSN
